title: Storming of the Bastille
summary: On the afternoon of 14 July 1789, after a morning spent seizing thirty-two thousand muskets from the Hôtel des Invalides, a crowd of Parisian workers, tradesmen, and mutinying French Guards forced the surrender of the medieval fortress-prison of the Bastille in the faubourg Saint-Antoine. About ninety-eight of them were killed; the governor, Bernard-René de Launay, was dragged to the Hôtel de Ville and stabbed to death. Only seven prisoners were freed. The military event was tiny; the political shock was enormous, and 14 July has been remembered ever since as the day the French Revolution became irreversible.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the summer of 1789 the Bourbon monarchy was politically bankrupt in every sense. The French treasury, which had funded the American Revolution to spite the British, faced a deficit of some 126 million livres against an annual revenue of about 475 million; interest on the accumulated debt consumed roughly half of state expenditure. The finance minister Charles Alexandre de Calonne's reform proposals of 1786 had been rejected by an Assembly of Notables in 1787; his successor Étienne Charles de Loménie de Brienne had failed the same year to force new taxes past the parlements of Paris and the provinces. In August 1788 the king, Louis XVI, agreed to convene the Estates-General, the ancient consultative body of clergy, nobility, and commons that had not met since 1614.

The crisis was aggravated by two winters of bad weather. A freak hailstorm on 13 July 1788 destroyed the harvest across a broad swathe of northern France; the winter of 1788 to 1789 was the coldest in a century; bread prices in Paris in July 1789 stood roughly fifty per cent above the January average, consuming as much as ninety per cent of a labourer's income. The Estates-General met at Versailles on 5 May 1789 in an atmosphere of hopeful, desperate expectation. It quickly deadlocked over voting procedure. On 17 June the Third Estate declared itself the National Assembly, joined three days later at the Tennis Court by clergy and some nobles in the famous oath not to disperse until France had a constitution.

The king, hesitating between conciliation and repression, chose both. On 27 June he ordered the recalcitrant orders to join the Third Estate in the National Assembly, but he simultaneously ordered twenty-five thousand foreign troops, mostly Swiss and German mercenary regiments loyal to the crown, to concentrate around Paris and Versailles. On 11 July he dismissed Jacques Necker, the Genevan Protestant finance minister who had come to symbolise reform, and replaced him with a hardline conservative ministry under the Baron de Breteuil.

News of Necker's dismissal reached Paris on the afternoon of Sunday 12 July. In the gardens of the Palais-Royal, the property of the king's cousin the duc d'Orléans, a young journalist named Camille Desmoulins jumped on a table and called the crowd to arms, warning that troops were preparing a Saint Bartholomew's Day for patriots. Riots spread. On 13 July an improvised municipal committee formed at the Hôtel de Ville authorised the creation of a bourgeois militia of forty-eight thousand men and set about finding weapons. That night crowds ransacked customs barriers around the city and pillaged the monastery of Saint-Lazare for grain.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On the morning of Tuesday 14 July the search for arms turned decisive. A crowd of perhaps sixty thousand marched to the Hôtel des Invalides on the Left Bank, where the aged marshal de Sombreuil surrendered the arsenal without a serious fight. Contemporary accounts credit the crowd with carrying off some thirty-two thousand muskets and twenty cannon; the muskets lacked powder and shot, which were held not at the Invalides but in the storerooms of the medieval fortress on the eastern edge of the city, the Bastille.

The Bastille was a squat, eight-towered stone rectangle built between 1370 and 1383 as part of the eastern defences of Paris. Under Louis XIV it had become the state prison for political detainees, though by 1789 its role had shrunk. On 14 July it held only seven prisoners: four forgers, two lunatics, and one aristocrat locked up at his family's request. The garrison consisted of eighty-two invalides (aged veterans) and thirty-two Swiss soldiers of the Salis-Samade Regiment who had been reinforced only a week earlier. The commander was the fifty-year-old marquis Bernard-René Jourdan de Launay, born in the fortress and now, unhappily, its governor.

A delegation from the Hôtel de Ville, led by the merchant Jacques-Alexis Thuriot, arrived at the Bastille around ten in the morning to negotiate the surrender of the gunpowder stored there. De Launay received them courteously and invited them to lunch. Outside, the crowd grew impatient. Around one in the afternoon a small group cut the chains of the outer drawbridge and forced their way into the outer courtyard. De Launay's men, feeling they were under attack, opened fire; casualties mounted rapidly among the crowd, which pulled back and demanded reinforcements from the mutinying French Guards. Two detachments of the Guards arrived in the mid-afternoon under Pierre-Augustin Hulin and Jacob Élie, bringing five cannon captured from the Invalides. The guns were positioned in the outer court and pointed at the main drawbridge.

By five in the afternoon de Launay, seeing that the guns would make short work of the drawbridge chains, wrote a note offering to surrender if the garrison could march out with the honours of war. His officers refused to accept the terms; he then wrote a second note threatening to blow up the powder magazine (twenty thousand pounds of gunpowder that would have flattened much of the faubourg) unless he were allowed to leave. Neither note was accepted. Around five-thirty he ordered the drawbridge lowered and the fortress surrendered. About ninety-eight of the attackers had been killed and seventy-three wounded; the garrison had lost one killed during the fighting.

De Launay and his officers were marched under guard toward the Hôtel de Ville, protected by the French Guards who had led the assault. The crowd along the rue Saint-Antoine grew more violent; de Launay, exhausted and terrified, is said to have kicked a cook named Desnot in the groin as they neared the Place de Grève. Desnot and others fell on him with knives and bayonets. His severed head, along with those of the lieutenant-major de Losme-Salbray and three of the veteran garrison, was mounted on a pike and paraded through the streets. That evening the seven bewildered prisoners were released; the count Jourdan, the last commander of a fortress that was already being taken to pieces stone by stone, was dead.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The political effect of the Bastille was immediate. On the morning of 15 July Louis XVI, without troops on which he could rely (many soldiers had already deserted), walked to the National Assembly at Versailles and announced that he had ordered the concentrated regiments withdrawn. On 16 July Jacques Necker was recalled from exile. On 17 July the king rode to the Hôtel de Ville in Paris, accepted a revolutionary tricolour cockade (adding the red and blue of Paris to the white of the Bourbons) from the mayor Jean-Sylvain Bailly, and formally recognised the new municipal government. The marquis de Lafayette, hero of the American War, was named commander of the newly formed Garde nationale, which absorbed and disciplined the militias of 12 to 14 July.

Outside Paris the news detonated a summer of rural revolt known as the Great Fear. From roughly 20 July to 5 August peasants across France, spurred by wildly exaggerated rumours of aristocratic conspiracies and marauding bandits, attacked chateaux, burnt seigneurial archives, and forced local lords to renounce feudal dues. On the night of 4 August 1789, at a legendary sitting of the National Assembly, nobles rose in cascade to renounce their tithes, tax exemptions, and seigneurial rights, and by dawn the entire structure of French feudal privilege had been formally abolished. On 26 August the Assembly adopted the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, drafted by Lafayette in consultation with Thomas Jefferson (then American minister in Paris), which recast the state as founded on the equal rights of citizens.

Demolition of the Bastille began within a week of its fall. The entrepreneur Pierre-François Palloy took the contract; over the following five months the fortress was reduced to a heap of stones. Palloy turned some of the stones into scale-model Bastilles that were sent to the eighty-three new departments as revolutionary relics, and turned others into commemorative medals and inkstands. The Place de la Bastille, empty since 1790, remained an open square; the column that stands there today commemorates a later revolution, that of July 1830.

Most of the actors of 14 July had short lives. Camille Desmoulins was guillotined in 1794 during the Terror. Bailly was guillotined the same year. Necker resigned again in 1790 and retired to Switzerland. Lafayette fled France in 1792 and spent five years in Austrian prisons before returning to become a symbol of the liberal opposition under the Bourbon Restoration. Louis XVI, who had written a single word in his hunting journal for 14 July ('rien', 'nothing'), was tried by the National Convention in December 1792 and guillotined on 21 January 1793.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
14 July became the national day of the French Republic through a long and interrupted process. The first anniversary in 1790 was celebrated as the Fête de la Fédération on the Champ de Mars, a mass ceremony of federation delegates from the departments swearing loyalty to the constitution. The Restoration and Second Empire suppressed the memory. Only in 1880, under the Third Republic, was 14 July formally established as the fête nationale; the choice was deliberately ambiguous, honouring both 1789 and the 1790 Fête. It has been celebrated with military parades on the Champs-Elysées, republican speeches, and provincial dances ever since, most conspicuously at the bicentennial of 1989.

Beyond France the storming became one of the founding images of the modern revolutionary tradition. The Marseillaise, composed in Strasbourg in 1792 and adopted as the national anthem, anchored a musical language of revolution that would echo from Haiti to Ho Chi Minh. Karl Marx, writing in the middle nineteenth century, saw 1789 as the classic bourgeois revolution, the pattern from which the proletarian revolution would later emerge. In the twentieth century, from the Russian February to the Cuban and Iranian revolutions, insurrectionary crowds have repeatedly been compared, by their friends and by their enemies, to the sans-culottes of the faubourg Saint-Antoine.

Historians have long since punctured the mythology. The Bastille of 1789 was not the medieval prison of a thousand nameless victims: it was a much reduced fortress in the middle of a demolition proposal, and the seven prisoners freed on 14 July hardly justified the sacrifice of ninety-eight lives. But the political fact remains that the fall of the Bastille demonstrated, for the first time in centuries, that royal authority in France could be beaten on the streets by ordinary Parisians. In that sense the day did what it was supposed to: it made the Revolution possible. Every 14 July since has been a rehearsal of that recognition, and a reminder that political sovereignty in the modern world has often begun with a crowd, a rumour, and a fortress that turned out to be less impregnable than it looked.

worldContext.text: In North America the young United States had just inaugurated George Washington as first president in New York on 30 April 1789; the first Congress was drafting the ten amendments that would become the Bill of Rights (proposed in September 1789, ratified in 1791). Louisiana and the Mississippi valley belonged to Spain. In Spanish America the great Tupac Amaru II rebellion of 1780 to 1783 was still fresh in memory, and Bourbon reforms were straining relations with creole elites. In Portuguese Brazil the Inconfidência Mineira of 1789, a small independence conspiracy at Vila Rica, was uncovered in April and its leaders arrested.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdulhamid I (who died on 7 April 1789) and then Selim III was in the middle of another disastrous war with Habsburg Austria and Russia; the Russians had taken Ochakov in December 1788 and would take Bender in 1789. Selim III, aged twenty-seven, began his reign with an ambitious programme of military reform, the Nizam-i Cedid. In Egypt the Mamluk beys Murad and Ibrahim ruled Cairo in near-independence; Napoleon's expedition was nine years off.

The Qianlong Emperor of Qing China was in the fifty-fourth year of his reign, an old man of seventy-eight ruling the wealthiest state in the world through an increasingly corrupt court dominated by the favourite Heshen. In 1788 to 1789 Qing armies fought an inconclusive campaign in the mountains of Vietnam supporting the deposed Le dynasty against the Tay Son rebellion; the Tay Son under Nguyen Hue crushed a Qing army at Ngoc Hoi outside Hanoi at Chinese New Year 1789. In Tokugawa Japan the shogun Tokugawa Ienari had just begun the Kansei Reforms of 1787.

In India the third Anglo-Mysore War was about to erupt: Tipu Sultan of Mysore invaded the British-allied kingdom of Travancore in December 1789. The British East India Company under Governor-General Charles Cornwallis (who had surrendered at Yorktown eight years before) was now the dominant power in the subcontinent, though the Marathas still controlled much of central and western India and the Sikh Confederacy was consolidating the Punjab. Nepal under the young king Rana Bahadur Shah was expanding into the Himalayan foothills.

The trans-Atlantic slave trade had reached its historical peak: Britain, France, Portugal, and other European powers were shipping perhaps eighty thousand enslaved Africans a year across the ocean, most to Caribbean and Brazilian plantations. In West Africa the Asante Empire dominated the Gold Coast; the Kingdom of Dahomey traded slaves and firearms. In the Cape Colony Dutch settlers were pressing further inland; a first British invasion (from 1795) was still six years off. In the Horn of Africa the Solomonic empire of Ethiopia had entered its long Zamana Masafent, or era of princes.

The First Fleet of British convicts under Captain Arthur Phillip had landed at Sydney Cove on 26 January 1788, establishing the colony of New South Wales that would grow into modern Australia. In April 1789 the mutiny on HMS Bounty had occurred in the mid-Pacific; Fletcher Christian's mutineers would settle Pitcairn Island in January 1790. In Hawaii the young chief Kamehameha was beginning the wars that would unify the archipelago; in Aotearoa Māori communities had not yet felt the impact of the whalers and sealers who would arrive in the 1790s.

figures.name: Bernard-René Jourdan de Launay
figures.role: Governor of the Bastille, killed by the crowd after surrendering the fortress

figures.name: Louis XVI
figures.role: King of France, who wrote 'rien' in his hunting journal for 14 July 1789

figures.name: Jacques Necker
figures.role: Genevan Protestant finance minister whose dismissal on 11 July triggered the July insurrection

figures.name: Camille Desmoulins
figures.role: Journalist whose call to arms at the Palais-Royal on 12 July mobilised the Parisian crowd

figures.name: Marquis de Lafayette
figures.role: Veteran of the American Revolution, appointed commander of the new Paris National Guard on 15 July

images.caption: The Storming of the Bastille, 14 July 1789, gouache by the eyewitness Jean-Pierre Houel showing the assault on the outer courtyard and the surrender of the garrison.
images.credit: Jean-Pierre Houël (1735 to 1813), via Wikimedia Commons
